Keywords: problem statement, scope, boundaries, criteria, stakeholders, constraints, framing, reframing
Description: Craft a precise problem statement that is specific, bounded, actionable, and measurable. The quality of the solution depends entirely on how well the problem is defined.
Key Activities: Write problem statement worksheet; Identify decision maker and stakeholders; Set success criteria; Define scope boundaries; Establish time and resource constraints; Challenge initial framing
Common Pitfalls: Solving the wrong problem; Too broad or too narrow scope; Accepting the problem as given without questioning; Confusing symptoms with root causes; Skipping stakeholder alignment
Output Artifacts: Problem statement worksheet; Scope boundary document; Success criteria list; Stakeholder map
Quality Gate: Problem statement passes the 'so what' test and is specific enough to decompose
Tips: Use the 'what would you need to believe' test; Ask 'why' 5 times to find root cause; Restate the problem 3 different ways to find the best framing

Keywords: logic tree, MECE, decomposition, issue tree, hypothesis tree, breakdown, structure, cleaving
Description: Break the problem into component parts using logic trees that are MECE (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive). This reveals the key drivers and creates a roadmap for analysis.
Key Activities: Build issue tree or hypothesis tree; Apply MECE principle; Identify cleaving frames; Test different decomposition approaches; Ensure leaves are analyzable; Validate structure with team
Common Pitfalls: Not being MECE; Stopping at too high a level; Using only one decomposition approach; Making the tree too deep before testing; Confusing issues with solutions
Output Artifacts: Logic tree (issue or hypothesis); MECE validation; Cleaving frame selection; Prioritized branches
Quality Gate: Every branch is MECE; Leaf nodes are specific enough to analyze individually; Tree captures at least 80% of the problem
Tips: Try at least 2 different decompositions before committing; Start with the broadest cleave; Each leaf should suggest a specific analysis

Keywords: 80/20, pareto, prioritization, impact, feasibility, sensitivity, focus, triage, screening
Description: Not all branches of the tree matter equally. Focus effort on the issues that will have the most impact on the answer. Apply the 80/20 rule ruthlessly.
Key Activities: Rank branches by potential impact on answer; Assess feasibility of analysis; Cross off branches that wont change the conclusion; Identify quick wins vs deep dives; Estimate sensitivity of each factor
Common Pitfalls: Spreading effort too thin; Analyzing everything equally; Ignoring the 80/20 rule; Letting personal interest drive priorities instead of impact; Analysis paralysis
Output Artifacts: Prioritized issue list; Impact-feasibility matrix; Pruned logic tree; Analysis priority ranking
Quality Gate: Top 2-3 issues identified that drive 80% of the answer; Clear rationale for deprioritizing other branches
Tips: Ask 'if this branch turns out to be true/false, does it change my recommendation?'; Use dot-voting with team for quick prioritization

Keywords: workplan, analysis plan, hypotheses, data sources, timeline, responsibilities, milestones
Description: Design the analyses needed to resolve each priority issue. For each issue, define what analysis to do, what data to gather, who is responsible, and by when.
Key Activities: Map each priority issue to specific analyses; Define data requirements; Assign responsibilities; Set milestones and deadlines; Plan for iteration; Identify early deliverables
Common Pitfalls: Over-planning without doing; Not linking analyses to specific hypotheses; Ignoring data availability; Sequential planning when parallel is possible; No contingency for surprises
Output Artifacts: Workplan matrix (issue > hypothesis > analysis > data > owner > deadline); Data source inventory; Timeline with milestones
Quality Gate: Every priority issue has at least one mapped analysis; Data sources identified and accessible; Timeline is realistic with built-in iteration points
Tips: Use hypothesis-driven approach: state what you believe and design tests; Front-load the hardest analyses; Plan for a Day 1 answer (best guess) and iterate from there

Keywords: data analysis, hypothesis testing, benchmarking, interviews, modeling, experiments, evidence, insights
Description: Execute the workplan. Gather data, run analyses, test hypotheses. Be rigorous but efficient - use the simplest analysis that answers the question.
Key Activities: Execute planned analyses; Gather and clean data; Test hypotheses against evidence; Iterate when results surprise; Triangulate from multiple sources; Document findings and confidence levels
Common Pitfalls: Boiling the ocean with data; Confirmation bias in analysis; Not iterating on surprising results; Using complex methods when simple ones suffice; Treating correlation as causation
Output Artifacts: Analysis results; Data tables and charts; Hypothesis validation/invalidation; Confidence assessments; Key insights per branch
Quality Gate: Each priority hypothesis has been tested; Results are documented with confidence levels; Surprising findings have been investigated further
Tips: Start with the 'Day 1 answer' - your best hypothesis; Use the simplest analysis first; Triangulate important findings; Be explicit about what you dont know

Keywords: synthesis, so-what, insight, conclusion, recommendation, grouping, pattern, pyramid, storyline
Description: Pull insights together into a coherent story. Move from analyses to 'so what' implications. Group findings into themes and build the logical argument for your recommendation.
Key Activities: Extract key insights from each analysis; Apply 'so what?' test to every finding; Group insights into themes (situation-complication-resolution or other); Build governing thought; Construct pyramid of arguments; Identify risks and mitigations
Common Pitfalls: Reporting all findings without synthesis; Missing the 'so what'; Not having a clear governing thought; Burying the lead; Confusing data presentation with insight; Not addressing counterarguments
Output Artifacts: Synthesis pyramid; Governing thought statement; Grouped insights with implications; Risk assessment; Recommendation with rationale
Quality Gate: Clear governing thought that answers the original problem; Every key finding has a 'so what'; Recommendation is supported by evidence pyramid; Risks acknowledged with mitigations
Tips: Write the one-sentence answer first; Then build support below it; Test your synthesis by asking 'would a skeptic be convinced?'; Address the strongest counterargument directly

Keywords: presentation, storytelling, pyramid principle, action, audience, persuasion, executive summary, visualization
Description: Present findings and recommendations in a way that drives action. Tailor the communication to the audience. Lead with the answer, support with evidence.
Key Activities: Tailor message to audience; Lead with the recommendation (pyramid principle); Support with evidence and analysis; Anticipate objections; Include clear next steps; Choose the right medium (memo, presentation, conversation)
Common Pitfalls: Burying the recommendation at the end; Too much detail for the audience; Not anticipating pushback; No clear call to action; Using jargon with non-technical audiences; Death by PowerPoint
Output Artifacts: Executive summary; Presentation deck or memo; One-page summary; Implementation roadmap; Q&A preparation document
Quality Gate: Audience understands and can act on the recommendation; Key message fits in one sentence; Supporting evidence is clear and compelling; Next steps are specific and assigned
Tips: Practice the 'elevator pitch' version; For executive audiences: recommendation first, with backup slides; Use the 'Minto Pyramid' - answer > supporting arguments > detailed evidence
